findings.title: POST /tasks/bulk/update passes unvalidated `changes` to updateMany
findings.detail: The `ids` array is length-checked, but `changes` is destructured from raw JSON and passed directly to `services.taskService.updateMany(ids, changes)` without Zod safeParse. An attacker could inject arbitrary fields (e.g., `id`, `createdAt`) that the service layer may blindly merge. Should validate `changes` against `UpdateTaskInput.safeParse()`.
findings.impact: Medium — could corrupt task data by overwriting protected fields. Mitigated by better-sqlite3 ignoring unknown columns, but Markdown backend merges all fields.

findings.title: PUT /settings/:key accepts any value type via `body.value as string`
findings.detail: The settings PUT endpoint casts `body.value as string` without type checking. If `body.value` is an object, number, or null, it will be stringified incorrectly or stored as 'undefined'. The key allowlist prevents writes to sensitive keys, but there is no schema validation on the value itself. Should add `z.object({ value: z.string() }).safeParse(body)`.
findings.impact: Low — the key allowlist limits blast radius, but malformed values could break UI settings parsing.

findings.title: POST /tasks/reorder and POST /tasks/import lack body validation
findings.detail: `/reorder` destructures `orderedIds` without checking it is a string array. `/import` destructures `importedTasks` without validating the array or individual items. Both should have Zod schemas. Lower severity because the import loop already has per-item try/catch and reorder delegates to TaskService which validates internally.
findings.impact: Low — graceful failures exist, but explicit validation would give better error messages.

findings.title: Several AI/plugin endpoints use `as` type assertions instead of Zod
findings.detail: PUT /ai/config, POST models/load, POST models/unload, PUT sessions/:id/title, PUT memories/:id all destructure via `as { ... }` casts. These are internal-facing endpoints (the AI config, model load/unload), and most have secondary validation (e.g., isAllowedBaseUrl). Low priority but inconsistent with the Zod validation pattern used elsewhere.
findings.impact: Low — all are internal-facing; the app is local-first with no external network exposure by default.
